name: New Mexico
examName: New Mexico Real Estate Broker Examination

examFacts.notes: Administered by PSI for the New Mexico Real Estate Commission (NMREC). The 75-question national portion and the 50-question New Mexico state-law portion are scored separately, and you need 75% on each.

intro:
New Mexico's real estate exam pairs 75 national questions with a hefty 50-question state-law portion, each scored separately at 75%. The New Mexico Real Estate Commission (NMREC) contracts with PSI to administer it as a split test, 75 scored national questions and 50 scored New Mexico state-law questions, with two hours allowed for the national portion and one hour for the state portion. New Mexico uses the word "broker" for what most other states call a salesperson license, so the very first license you earn is an associate broker license and the exam covers full broker scope from day one. The two portions are scored separately and you need 75 percent on each, so a strong national score won't rescue a thin grasp of New Mexico law, which is exactly why New Mexico-specific preparation matters as much as the national material.

State-specific prep matters more here than almost anywhere else. New Mexico tests its own rules in ways a national course never touches, from the mandatory written broker duties every licensee owes to every party in a transaction, to the brokerage relationship choices of exclusive agency, dual agency, and transaction broker, the qualifying broker who supervises every associate broker in an office, the community property rules that decide how married couples take title, prior-appropriation water rights, the required Public Improvement District and estimated property tax disclosures, and the New Mexico Human Rights Act with its broader list of protected classes. None of that looks like the material a candidate in a neighboring state studies. A generic real estate practice test won't cut it. You need New Mexico-specific practice questions.

Below are 20 free New Mexico real estate test questions and answers from RealReady's full New Mexico bank. Each tests a specific topic with the correct answer and a plain-English explanation under the card. Read each one, commit to an answer, then reveal. Treat it like a real NMREC practice test, and the ones you miss show you what to focus on next.

Get the RealReady app on iPhone, iPad, or Android for the rest of the New Mexico question bank. The full app includes short articles that walk you through the why behind each topic, detailed explanations on every question, a missed-question drill mode, and progress tracking that shows your per-category accuracy.

faq.q: How many questions are on the New Mexico real estate broker exam?
faq.a: The New Mexico Real Estate Broker Examination has 125 scored multiple-choice questions, split into a 75-question national portion and a 50-question New Mexico state-law portion. PSI also mixes in five to ten unscored pretest questions on each portion that look identical to the scored ones, so you should answer everything.

faq.q: What's the passing score for the New Mexico real estate broker exam?
faq.a: You need a score of 75 percent on each portion, and the national and state portions are scored separately. If you pass one portion and fail the other, you only retake the portion you failed, but you have to pass both within 90 calendar days of your first attempt or you start over with both halves.

faq.q: How much does the New Mexico real estate broker exam cost?
faq.a: The PSI examination fee is $95 per attempt, paid to the testing company when you schedule your seat. That single fee covers the exam whether you sit for one portion or both. After you pass, the associate broker license application fee paid to the New Mexico Real Estate Commission is $270.

faq.q: How long is the New Mexico real estate broker exam?
faq.a: Three hours of seat time total. PSI allows two hours for the 75-question national portion and one hour for the 50-question New Mexico portion, taken in one sitting. You get your official pass or fail score report before you leave the test center.

faq.q: Who administers the New Mexico real estate exam?
faq.a: The New Mexico Real Estate Broker Examination is administered by PSI for the New Mexico Real Estate Commission (NMREC). You register and schedule your exam date through PSI.

faq.q: Is the New Mexico real estate broker exam hard?
faq.a: Yes, more than most candidates expect. New Mexico is one of a smaller group of states where the entry-level license is the broker license, so the exam tests full broker scope from the start, including the brokerage relationships, the written broker duties disclosure, trust accounts, and the qualifying broker supervision structure rather than just salesperson basics. The 50-question state portion is dense with license law and rule, and a single missed concept on something like the broker duties owed to every party or a required disclosure can pull you under the 75 percent line. Honest practice on New Mexico-specific questions, not generic national content, is what gets people across the line on the first try.

faq.q: What's on the New Mexico real estate broker exam?
faq.a: The 75-question national portion covers property ownership and land use, valuation and market analysis, financing, general principles of agency, mandated disclosures, contracts, transfer of title, the practice of real estate, and real estate math. The 50-question New Mexico portion covers the Real Estate Commission's structure and disciplinary powers, the license law and Commission rules under 16.61 NMAC, the mandatory broker duties and brokerage relationships, required disclosures including stigmatized property and Public Improvement Districts, community property and how married couples take title, prior-appropriation water rights, trust accounts and earnest money, and the New Mexico Human Rights Act. New Mexico license law and brokerage relationships carry the heaviest weight on the state portion.

faq.q: What's the best way to prepare for the New Mexico real estate broker exam?
faq.a: After you finish your 90-hour broker pre-licensing coursework, the highest-leverage move is grinding New Mexico-specific practice questions with honest feedback on every miss. National principles you can pick up from any course, but the 50-question state portion rewards repetition on the license law, the written broker duties, the brokerage relationship choices, trust account handling, and New Mexico's community property and water rights rules. The RealReady app gives you the full New Mexico question bank plus the national bank, detailed explanations, a missed-question drill mode, and per-category progress tracking so you can see exactly where you're weak. Most users study 30 to 60 minutes a day for two to four weeks before sitting the exam.

The 20 New Mexico practice questions on this page are free, no account or signup needed. Unlocking the rest of the New Mexico bank is a single in-app purchase you pay once and own, with no recurring charge and no time limit on access.

faq.q: Does RealReady cover the New Mexico real estate exam?
faq.a: Yes. RealReady has a dedicated New Mexico question bank built for the New Mexico real estate exam, covering the national principles every candidate sees plus the New Mexico law that trips people up, like the mandatory written broker duties, the qualifying broker supervision structure, and the New Mexico Human Rights Act protected classes.

faq.q: Is RealReady a real estate license course?
faq.a: No. RealReady is exam practice, not a pre-license course, so it doesn't count toward New Mexico's required pre-license education hours. Use it after or alongside your coursework to drill realistic practice questions and find your weak spots before exam day.
